name: [TEST FIXTURE] WHO First Aid Manual

guides.title: CPR for adults
guides.content:
[TEST FIXTURE — placeholder content, not medically reviewed. Do not rely on this in a real emergency. Call your local emergency number.]

If an adult is unresponsive and not breathing, call for help and begin compressions. Place the heel of one hand on the center of the chest, the other hand on top, and push down at least 2 inches at a rate of 100-120 per minute. Allow the chest to fully recoil between compressions. Keep going until trained help takes over or the person responds.

If you are trained and willing, give 2 rescue breaths after every 30 compressions. If untrained or uncomfortable, compression-only CPR is the recommended fallback.
guides.sourceRef: [TEST FIXTURE] AUTO-GENERATED — no real source. Real authoring under OFF-1373..1378 will replace this with a CDC/FEMA/NIH/etc citation.

guides.title: CPR for children and infants
guides.content:
[TEST FIXTURE — placeholder content. Real pediatric CPR requires technique adjustments for age and size. Take a certified course.]

For children (1 year to puberty), use one or two hands depending on body size. Compress to about one third the depth of the chest. For infants under 1 year, use two fingers in the center of the chest, again to one third of chest depth. Cycle remains 30 compressions to 2 breaths if you are alone. If a second person is present, switch to 15:2 for infants and children.
guides.sourceRef: [TEST FIXTURE] AUTO-GENERATED — no real source. Real authoring under OFF-1373..1378 will replace this with a CDC/FEMA/NIH/etc citation.

guides.title: Choking — adults and children
guides.content:
[TEST FIXTURE — placeholder. Consult a real first-aid manual for full technique.]

If someone can cough or speak, encourage them to cough. If they cannot, alternate 5 back blows between the shoulder blades with 5 abdominal thrusts (just above the navel, in and up). Continue until the object clears or the person loses consciousness, at which point start CPR.

For infants, alternate 5 back blows with 5 chest thrusts using two fingers. Do not perform abdominal thrusts on infants.
guides.sourceRef: [TEST FIXTURE] AUTO-GENERATED — no real source. Real authoring under OFF-1373..1378 will replace this with a CDC/FEMA/NIH/etc citation.

guides.title: Severe bleeding control
guides.content:
[TEST FIXTURE — placeholder.]

Apply firm direct pressure with a clean cloth, gauze, or your hand. Maintain pressure for at least 10 uninterrupted minutes. Do not lift the cloth to check — add more on top. Elevate the wound above the heart if possible.

If bleeding does not stop and is life-threatening, apply a tourniquet 2-3 inches above the wound (never on a joint), tighten until bleeding stops, and write the time on the patient's forehead or visible skin.
guides.sourceRef: [TEST FIXTURE] AUTO-GENERATED — no real source. Real authoring under OFF-1373..1378 will replace this with a CDC/FEMA/NIH/etc citation.

guides.title: Burns — assessment and care
guides.content:
[TEST FIXTURE — placeholder.]

Cool the burn under cool (not cold) running water for 20 minutes. Do not apply ice, butter, or toothpaste. Remove jewelry near the burn before swelling sets in. Cover loosely with a clean, non-stick dressing.

Seek emergency care for: burns larger than your palm, burns to the face/hands/feet/groin, electrical or chemical burns, or any burn on a child or older adult. Third-degree burns (white, leathery, or charred) always need professional care.
guides.sourceRef: [TEST FIXTURE] AUTO-GENERATED — no real source. Real authoring under OFF-1373..1378 will replace this with a CDC/FEMA/NIH/etc citation.

guides.title: Recognizing and treating shock
guides.content:
[TEST FIXTURE — placeholder.]

Shock can follow severe injury, blood loss, allergic reaction, or heart problems. Signs include pale or clammy skin, rapid weak pulse, confusion, rapid shallow breathing, and weakness.

Lay the person down and elevate their legs about 12 inches unless there is a head, neck, back, or leg injury. Keep them warm with a blanket. Do not give food or water. Get emergency help fast — shock is life-threatening.
guides.sourceRef: [TEST FIXTURE] AUTO-GENERATED — no real source. Real authoring under OFF-1373..1378 will replace this with a CDC/FEMA/NIH/etc citation.

guides.title: Stroke — the FAST check
guides.content:
[TEST FIXTURE — placeholder.]

Use the FAST acronym to recognize a stroke:

Face — ask the person to smile. Does one side droop?
Arms — ask them to raise both arms. Does one drift downward?
Speech — ask them to repeat a simple sentence. Is it slurred or strange?
Time — if any of these are present, call emergency services immediately. Note the time symptoms started; treatment is time-critical.
guides.sourceRef: [TEST FIXTURE] AUTO-GENERATED — no real source. Real authoring under OFF-1373..1378 will replace this with a CDC/FEMA/NIH/etc citation.

guides.title: Severe allergic reaction (anaphylaxis)
guides.content:
[TEST FIXTURE — placeholder.]

Signs: hives, swelling of face/throat, difficulty breathing, rapid pulse, dizziness, vomiting. Can be fatal within minutes.

If the person has an epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen), help them use it immediately — into the outer thigh, holding for 3 seconds. Call emergency services even if symptoms improve; a second wave can follow. Have them lie flat with legs raised unless they are vomiting or having trouble breathing.
guides.sourceRef: [TEST FIXTURE] AUTO-GENERATED — no real source. Real authoring under OFF-1373..1378 will replace this with a CDC/FEMA/NIH/etc citation.
